result:
# The Strange Comfort of Unfinished Things

There's something I find genuinely interesting about objects left mid-process. A half-read book splayed open on a nightstand. A puzzle with a stubborn gap in the upper corner. A song that fades out rather than resolving to its final chord.

We tend to treat incompleteness as failure, as evidence of distraction or laziness. But I think unfinished things carry a particular kind of energy that completed ones simply don't have. They remain *alive* somehow. The open book still has somewhere to go. The puzzle still has a problem to solve. The fading song leaves the listener doing invisible work, mentally reaching for the resolution that never arrives.

Psychologists actually have a name for our tendency to remember incomplete tasks better than finished ones — the Zeigarnik effect. Our minds apparently keep a kind of open tab on unresolved things, returning to them like a tongue finding a sore tooth.

But I wonder if this goes beyond mere cognitive quirk. Maybe the unfinished represents possibility in its purest form. A completed thing is what it is, fully determined, its potential collapsed into a single outcome. An unfinished thing still contains multitudes. It could still become several versions of itself.

There's obvious danger in romanticizing incompleteness too heavily. Abandoned projects litter most lives with genuine regret.

Still, I think there's wisdom in occasionally sitting with something unresolved and resisting the urge to force a conclusion. Some things earn their open endings.

The puzzle can wait until tomorrow.
